revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''内侍'''(ないし)は[[平安時代]]、[[後宮]]の役所[[内侍司]](ないしのつかさ)に勤めた、常時、奏請、伝宣等を掌る[[内侍司…'
revision.text: '''内侍'''(ないし)は[[平安時代]]、[[後宮]]の役所[[内侍司]](ないしのつかさ)に勤めた、常時、奏請、伝宣等を掌る[[内侍司]]の[[女官]]の総称。 上位から順に、[[尚侍]](ないしのかみ・しょうじ)、[[典侍]](ないしのすけ・てんじ)、[[掌侍]](ないしのじょう・しょうじ)、他に[[権掌侍]](ごんのないしじょう)｡

revision.comment: 加筆（但し、かなり手抜き）
revision.text:
{{加筆|地位･役職|date=2009年5月}}
'''内侍'''（ないし）は近代以前の[[日本]]の[[女性職]]の1種である。内侍には以下のものがある。
#[[律令制|令制下]]における、[[後宮]]の役所である[[内侍司]]（ないしのつかさ）に勤めた[[女官]]の総称。 #[[斎宮寮]]の女官の1種。 #[[斎院司]]の女官の1種。 #[[安芸国]]の[[厳島神社]]の[[巫女]]。
== 内侍司の内侍 == [[天皇]]に近侍して、常時天皇への奏上や、天皇からの宣下を仲介する等を職掌とした内侍司の女官の総称である。長官を[[尚侍]]（ないしのかみ / しょうじ）（定員2名）、次官を[[典侍]]（ないしのすけ/てんじ）（定員4名）、[[判官]]を[[掌侍]]（ないしのじょう / しょうじ）（定員4名）と称し、[[主典]]は欠くものの[[四等官制]]を採用していた。他に[[権掌侍]]（ごんのないしのじょう）（定員2名）が置かれることもあり、また内侍に障りがあって出仕できない場合などに「内侍代」が臨時に任命されることもあった。ちなみに[[春宮坊]]には「内侍代」が置かれ、即位の後にはそのまま内侍に就くことになっていた。 === 尚侍 === 詳しくは[[尚侍]]を参照。 === 典侍 === 常に天皇に近侍し、天皇が別殿に渡御する際には[[剣璽]]を捧持した。天皇の[[乳母]]が任ぜられることが多く、その場合は即位後に行われる[[八十島神祭]]の御使となる例であった。また新帝に譲位の場合、剣璽を[[近衛次将]]に送るのも典侍の勤めであったが（これを「'''送内侍'''（おくりないし）」といった）、これらの役職は後に掌侍の[[一﨟]]である「'''勾当'''（こうとう）'''内侍'''」がこれに代わるようになった。 === 掌侍 === 狭義における「内侍」は専らこの掌侍を指した｡
== 斎宮寮の内侍 == [[斎宮]]に近侍してその用を勤めた。
== 斎院司の内侍 == 斎宮寮に同じく、[[斎院|斎王]]に近侍してその用を勤めた。
== 厳島神社の内侍 == 厳島神社の女性神職（巫女）で、神事のほかに、同神社に[[参籠]]する貴人の旅情を慰めるために[[今様]]を朗詠したり[[舞楽]]などを行った。『[[厳島道芝記]]』の社家供僧内侍並諸役人神人之名の条には、一﨟から八﨟までの内侍の名を挙げ、これを「'''八乙女'''」と称し、また「'''本内侍'''」と称したことが見え、このほかにも「和琴内侍」や「韓神内侍」など、その数は十数人にも及んでいた。なお、[[平清盛]]の第7女で[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に召された[[御子姫君]]は、安芸守時代の清盛と、厳島内侍の間の子であるという。
== 参考文献 == *[[宮地直一]]・[[佐伯有義]]監修『神道大辞典 縮刷版』、[[臨川書店]]、昭和44年 ISBN 4-653-01347-0（初版は[[平凡社]]、昭和15年）
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ないし}} [[Category:身位]] [[Category:女性職]] [[Category:神職|*]]

revision.comment: /* 内侍司の内侍 */ 典侍を[[典侍]]へお引っ越し
revision.text:
{{加筆|地位･役職|date=2009年5月}}
'''内侍'''（ないし）は近代以前の[[日本]]の[[女性職]]の1種である。内侍には以下のものがある。
#[[律令制|令制下]]における、[[後宮]]の役所である[[内侍司]]（ないしのつかさ）に勤めた[[女官]]の総称。 #[[斎宮寮]]の女官の1種。 #[[斎院司]]の女官の1種。 #[[安芸国]]の[[厳島神社]]の[[巫女]]。

== 斎宮寮の内侍 == [[斎宮]]に近侍してその用を勤めた。
== 斎院司の内侍 == 斎宮寮に同じく、[[斎院|斎王]]に近侍してその用を勤めた。
== 厳島神社の内侍 == 厳島神社の女性神職（巫女）で、神事のほかに、同神社に[[参籠]]する貴人の旅情を慰めるために[[今様]]を朗詠したり[[舞楽]]などを行った。『[[厳島道芝記]]』の社家供僧内侍並諸役人神人之名の条には、一﨟から八﨟までの内侍の名を挙げ、これを「'''八乙女'''」と称し、また「'''本内侍'''」と称したことが見え、このほかにも「和琴内侍」や「韓神内侍」など、その数は十数人にも及んでいた。なお、[[平清盛]]の第7女で[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に召された[[御子姫君]]は、安芸守時代の清盛と、厳島内侍の間の子であるという。
== 参考文献 == *[[宮地直一]]・[[佐伯有義]]監修『神道大辞典 縮刷版』、[[臨川書店]]、昭和44年 ISBN 4-653-01347-0（初版は[[平凡社]]、昭和15年）
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ないし}} [[Category:身位]] [[Category:女性職]] [[Category:神職|*]]

長官を[[尚侍]]（ないしのかみ / しょうじ）（定員2名）、次官を[[典侍]]（ないしのすけ/てんじ）（定員4名）、[[判官]]を[[掌侍]]<ref>「内侍」とは第二義的に専らこの掌侍を指した。</ref>（ないしのじょう / しょうじ） （定員4名）と称し、[[主典]]は欠くものの[[四等官制]]を採用していた。他に'''権掌侍'''（ごんのないしのじょう）という[[権官]]が置かれることもあり（定員2名）、また内侍に障りがあって出仕できない場合などに'''内侍代'''が臨時に任命されることもあった。ちなみに[[春宮坊]]に内侍職は置かれなかったが、内侍代は常置され、即位の後にはそのまま内侍に就くことになっていた。
== 斎宮寮の内侍 == [[斎宮]]に近侍してその用を勤めた。
== 斎院司の内侍 == 斎宮寮に同じく、[[斎院|斎王]]に近侍してその用を勤めた。
== 厳島神社の内侍 == 厳島神社の女性神職（巫女）で、神事のほかに、同神社に[[参籠]]する貴人の旅情を慰めるために[[今様]]を朗詠したり[[舞楽]]などを行った。『[[厳島道芝記]]』の社家供僧内侍並諸役人神人之名の条には、一﨟から八﨟までの内侍の名を挙げ、これを「'''八乙女'''」と称し、また「'''本内侍'''」と称したことが見え、このほかにも「和琴内侍」や「韓神内侍」など、その数は十数人にも及んでいた。なお、[[平清盛]]の第7女で[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に召された[[御子姫君]]は、安芸守時代の清盛と、厳島内侍の間の子であるという。
== 参考文献 == *[[宮地直一]]・[[佐伯有義]]監修『神道大辞典 縮刷版』、[[臨川書店]]、昭和44年 ISBN 4-653-01347-0（初版は[[平凡社]]、昭和15年）
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ないし}} [[Category:身位]] [[Category:女官]] [[Category:斎宮]] [[Category:斎院]] [[Category:神職]]

{{加筆|地位･役職|date=2009年5月}}
'''内侍'''（ないし）は近代以前の[[日本]]の[[女性職]]の1種である。内侍には以下のものがある。

== 内侍司の内侍 == [[天皇]]に近侍して、常時天皇への奏上や、天皇からの宣下を仲介する等を職掌とした内侍司の女官の総称である。
長官を[[尚侍]]（ないしのかみ / しょうじ）（定員2名）、次官を[[典侍]]（ないしのすけ/てんじ）（定員4名）、[[判官]]を[[掌侍]]<ref>「内侍」とは第二義的に専らこの掌侍を指した。</ref>（ないしのじょう / しょうじ） （定員4名）と称し、[[主典]]は欠くものの[[四等官制]]を採用していた。他に'''権掌侍'''（ごんのないしのじょう）という[[権官]]が置かれることもあり（定員2名）、また内侍に障りがあって出仕できない場合などに'''内侍代'''が臨時に任命されることもあった。ちなみに[[春宮坊]]に内侍職は置かれなかったが、内侍代は常置され、即位の後にはそのまま内侍に就くことになっていた。
== 斎宮寮の内侍 == [[斎宮]]に近侍してその用を勤めた。
== 斎院司の内侍 == 斎宮寮に同じく、[[斎院|斎王]]に近侍してその用を勤めた。
== 厳島神社の内侍 == 厳島神社の女性神職（巫女）で、神事のほかに、同神社に[[参籠]]する貴人の旅情を慰めるために[[今様]]を朗詠したり[[舞楽]]などを行った。『[[厳島道芝記]]』の社家供僧内侍並諸役人神人之名の条には、一﨟から八﨟までの内侍の名を挙げ、これを「'''八乙女'''」と称し、また「'''本内侍'''」と称したことが見え、このほかにも「和琴内侍」や「韓神内侍」など、その数は十数人にも及んでいた。なお、[[平清盛]]の第7女で[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に召された[[御子姫君]]は、安芸守時代の清盛と、厳島内侍の間の子であるという。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *[[宮地直一]]・[[佐伯有義]]監修『神道大辞典 縮刷版』、[[臨川書店]]、昭和44年 ISBN 4-653-01347-0（初版は[[平凡社]]、昭和15年）

#[[律令制|令制下]]における、[[後宮]]の役所である[[内侍司]]（ないしのつかさ）に勤めた[[女官]]の総称。 #[[斎宮寮]]の女官の1種。 #[[斎院司]]の女官の1種。 #[[安芸国]]の[[厳島神社]]の[[巫女]]。

長官を[[尚侍]]（ないしのかみ / しょうじ）（定員2名）、次官を[[典侍]]（ないしのすけ/てんじ）（定員4名）、[[判官]]を[[掌侍]]<ref>「内侍」とは第二義的に専らこの掌侍を指した。</ref>（ないしのじょう / しょうじ） （定員4名）と称し、[[主典]]は欠くものの[[四等官制]]を採用していた。他に'''権掌侍'''（ごんのないしのじょう）という[[権官]]が置かれることもあり（定員2名）、また内侍に障りがあって出仕できない場合などに'''内侍代'''が臨時に任命されることもあった。ちなみに[[春宮坊]]に内侍職は置かれなかったが、内侍代は常置され、即位の後にはそのまま内侍に就くことになっていた。
== 斎宮寮の内侍 == [[斎宮]]に近侍してその用を務めた。
== 斎院司の内侍 == 斎宮寮に同じく、[[斎院|斎王]]に近侍してその用を務めた。
== 厳島神社の内侍 == 厳島神社の女性神職（巫女）で、神事のほかに、同神社に[[参籠]]する貴人の旅情を慰めるために[[今様]]を朗詠したり[[舞楽]]などを行った。『[[厳島道芝記]]』の社家供僧内侍並諸役人神人之名の条には、一﨟から八﨟までの内侍の名を挙げ、これを「'''八乙女'''」と称し、また「'''本内侍'''」と称したことが見え、このほかにも「和琴内侍」や「韓神内侍」など、その数は十数人にも及んでいた。なお、[[平清盛]]の第7女で[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に召された[[御子姫君]]は、安芸守時代の清盛と、[[厳島内侍]]の間の子であるという。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *[[宮地直一]]・[[佐伯有義]]監修『神道大辞典 縮刷版』、[[臨川書店]]、昭和44年 ISBN 4-653-01347-0（初版は[[平凡社]]、昭和15年）
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ないし}} [[Category:身位]] [[Category:日本の女官]] [[Category:斎宮]] [[Category:斎院]] [[Category:神道の称号・役職]]

revision.comment: Created page with "{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following: # The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮..."
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Japanese court ladies]] [[Category:Saikū]] [[Category:Saiin]] [[Category:Shinto titles and roles]] [[ja:内侍]]

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.

The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. == Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

[[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Japanese court ladies]] [[Category:Saikū]] [[Category:Saiin]] [[Category:Shinto titles and roles]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Court lady-qq]] [[Category:Court lady]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Court lady]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Court lady]] [[Category:sikyokyo]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:内侍]] [[Category:new pages]] [[ja:Court lady]] [[Category:new pages]] [[ja:Court lady]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Court lady]][[Category:qq]]

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} ''''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}

== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} ''''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} ''''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|平清盛}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} ''''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}}.

There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮}}}} ''''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院|12=draft}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王|12=draft}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社|12=draft}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女|12=draft}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様|12=draft}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽|12=draft}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君|12=draft}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori|12=draft}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮|12=draft}}}} ''''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職|12=draft}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司|12=draft}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮|12=draft}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司|12=draft}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女|12=draft}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国|12=draft}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司|12=draft}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇|12=draft}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上|12=draft}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下|12=draft}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|12=draft}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍|12=draft}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍|12=draft}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍|12=draft}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典|12=draft}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制|12=draft}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍|12=draft}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代|12=draft}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊|12=draft}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮|12=draft}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院|12=draft}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王|12=draft}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社|12=draft}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女|12=draft}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様|12=draft}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽|12=draft}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first rank (一﨟) to eighth rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君|12=draft}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori|12=draft}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[Miyachi Naoichi]] and [[Saeki Ariyoshi]] (supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Abridged Edition)|publisher=[[Rinsen Shoten]]|date=1969|isbn=4-653-01347-0|original=Heibonsha, 1940}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses|日本の内侍|朝鮮の内侍|宦官#朝鮮}} '''内侍'''（ないし）は近代以前の[[日本]]の[[女性職]]の1種である。内侍には以下のものがある。
#[[律令制|令制下]]における、[[後宮]]の役所である[[内侍司]]（ないしのつかさ）に勤めた[[女官]]の総称。 #[[斎宮寮]]の女官の1種。 #[[斎院司]]の女官の1種。 #[[安芸国]]の[[厳島神社]]の[[巫女]]。
== 内侍司の内侍 == [[天皇]]に近侍して、常時天皇への[[奏上]]や、天皇からの[[宣下]]を仲介すること及び[[賢所]]を管理すること等を職掌とした内侍司の女官の総称である。
長官を[[尚侍]]（ないしのかみ / しょうじ）（定員2名）、次官を[[典侍]]（ないしのすけ/てんじ）（定員4名）、[[判官]]を[[掌侍]]<ref>「内侍」とは第二義的に専らこの掌侍を指した。</ref>（ないしのじょう / しょうじ） （定員4名）と称し、[[主典]]は欠くものの[[四等官制]]を採用していた。他に'''{{Anchors|権掌侍}}権掌侍'''（ごんのないしのじょう）という[[権官]]が置かれることもあり（定員2名）、また内侍に障りがあって出仕できない場合などに'''内侍代'''が臨時に任命されることもあった。ちなみに[[春宮坊]]に内侍職は置かれなかったが、内侍代は常置され、即位の後にはそのまま内侍に就くことになっていた。
== 斎宮寮の内侍 == [[斎宮]]に近侍してその用を務めた。
== 斎院司の内侍 == 斎宮寮に同じく、[[斎院|斎王]]に近侍してその用を務めた。
== 厳島神社の内侍 == 厳島神社の女性神職（巫女）で、神事のほかに、同神社に[[参籠]]する貴人の旅情を慰めるために[[今様]]を朗詠したり[[舞楽]]などを行った。『[[厳島道芝記]]』の社家供僧内侍並諸役人神人之名の条には、一﨟から八﨟までの内侍の名を挙げ、これを「'''八乙女'''」と称し、また「'''本内侍'''」と称したことが見え、このほかにも「和琴内侍」や「韓神内侍」など、その数は十数人にも及んでいた。なお、[[平清盛]]の第7女で[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に召された[[御子姫君]]は、安芸守時代の清盛と、[[厳島内侍]]の間の子であるという。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *[[宮地直一]]・[[佐伯有義]]監修『神道大辞典 縮刷版』、[[臨川書店]]、昭和44年 ISBN 4-653-01347-0（初版は[[平凡社]]、昭和15年）
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ないし}} [[Category:身位]] [[Category:日本の女官]] [[Category:斎宮]] [[Category:斎院]] [[Category:神道の称号・役職]]

== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *[[宮地直一]]・[[佐伯有義]]監修『神道大辞典 縮刷版』、[[臨川書店]]、昭和44年 ISBN 4-653-01347-0（初版は[[平凡社]]、昭和15年）

Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imayō|qq=}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bugaku|qq=}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/厳島道芝記|qq=}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first Rank (一﨟) to eighth Rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikohimegimi|qq=}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kiyomori|qq=}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.

[[sn:Naishi (court lady)]] [[ja:内侍]] [[sn:ourt lady]] [[sn:Court lady]]
<!--Court lady-->

{{otheruses|Naishi in Japan|Naesi in Korea|{{ill|Eunuch#Korea|ja|宦官#朝鮮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eunuch#Korea|qq=}}}} '''Naishi''' (内侍) refers to a category of {{ill|female positions|ja|女性職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female positions|qq=}} in premodern [[Japan]]. The term "Naishi" encompasses the following:
# The female officials who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi-no-tsukasa|qq=}}, an office of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|qq=}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|qq=}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikū|qq=}}. # A type of female official in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiin|qq=}}. # A type of {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miko|qq=}} (shrine maiden) at the {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itsukushima Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aki Province|qq=}}.
== Naishi of the Naishi-no-tsukasa == The Naishi who served in the {{ill|Naishi-no-tsukasa|ja|内侍司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi-no-tsukasa|qq=}} were tasked with closely attending to the {{ill|Emperor|ja|天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor|qq=}}, handling duties such as mediating {{ill|sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōjō|qq=}} (reports to the emperor), conveying the emperor's {{ill|Senka|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senka|qq=}} (imperial decrees), and managing the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}}.
The head of the Naishi-no-tsukasa was called the {{ill|Shōji|ja|尚侍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōji|qq=}} (alternatively read as "Naishi-no-kami"), with a limit of two appointments. The second-Ranking officials were the {{ill|Tenji|ja|典侍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji|qq=}} (Naishi-no-suke), with four appointments, while the third-Ranking officials, known as {{ill|Shōji|ja|掌侍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōji|qq=}} (Naishi-no-jō), could also be referred to as "Naishi" in a narrower sense. There were four Shōji positions. Although there were no {{ill|Shuten|ja|主典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuten|qq=}} (clerks), the office followed a {{ill|Four-level official hierarchy|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-level official hierarchy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan制|qq=}}.
In addition, there were {{ill|Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|ja|権掌侍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-no-naishi-no-jō|qq=}} ("provisional Naishi-no-jō") positions with two appointments, as well as an ad hoc position called {{ill|Naishi-dai|ja|内侍代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi-dai|qq=}} to temporarily replace absent Naishi. Although no Naishi roles were directly assigned to the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|春宮坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=}}, the Naishi-dai was permanently stationed there and would typically transition to a full Naishi position upon the prince’s enthronement.
== Naishi of the Saikū == These Naishi served in close proximity to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikū|qq=}}, attending to her needs and responsibilities.
== Naishi of the Saiin == Similarly, the Naishi in the {{ill|Saiin|ja|斎院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiin|qq=}} served the {{ill|Saio|ja|斎王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saio|qq=}} in a comparable capacity to those of the Saikū.
== Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine == At {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itsukushima Shrine|qq=}}, the Naishi were female shrine priests ({{ill|Miko|ja|巫女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miko|qq=}}). Their duties included participating in rituals, reciting {{ill|Imayō|ja|今様|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imayō|qq=}} to entertain noble guests who lodged at the shrine, and performing {{ill|bugaku|ja|舞楽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bugaku|qq=}}. According to the "Itsukushima Michishiba-ki" ({{ill|厳島道芝記|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/厳島道芝記|qq=}}), the shrine maintained a hierarchical structure of Naishi from first Rank (一﨟) to eighth Rank (八﨟), collectively referred to as the "Yaotome" (八乙女) or "Hon-Naishi" (本内侍). Additional titles such as "Wagoto-Naishi" (和琴内侍) and "Karagami-Naishi" (韓神内侍) indicate a system of more than ten positions.
It is also said that {{ill|Mikohimegimi|ja|御子姫君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikohimegimi|qq=}}, the seventh daughter of {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|ja|Taira no Kiyomori|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kiyomori|qq=}}, was born to Kiyomori and a Naishi of Itsukushima Shrine during his tenure as the governor of Aki Province.
